questions.prompt: Why did SpaceX switch Falcon 9's grid fins from aluminum to titanium despite the mass penalty?

choices.text: Aluminum grid fins survived ascent but charred and warped during reentry, requiring replacement or significant refurbishment between flights — which broke the rapid-reuse economics. Titanium fins are heavier (~250 kg each, mounted high on the booster where mass is most expensive for ascent performance) but survive 20+ flights without refurbishment. The operational savings from durability across many flights vastly exceed the per-launch ascent performance cost. This is a recurring pattern in reusable hardware: choose for the operational tempo, not just the single-flight performance.

questions.explanation: Durability beats ascent performance when you're optimizing for fleet operations. Falcon 9's titanium fins, the stainless-steel Starship, and the increasingly hardened heat shields all reflect this principle — every part on a reusable vehicle must be designed for repeated cycles, not single use.

questions.prompt: Falcon 9 loses a single Merlin engine in the first stage during ascent. What happens?

choices.text: Mission usually succeeds. Falcon 9's nine-engine first stage is designed for engine-out tolerance: if one Merlin shuts down mid-ascent, the flight computer compensates by burning the remaining 8 engines longer and adjusting trajectory. CRS-1 in 2012 demonstrated this in practice — one Merlin shut down due to an anomaly, the rocket reached orbit anyway, and Dragon completed its ISS resupply mission. Losing 2 engines is tighter but probably still survivable. Losing the upper-stage Merlin Vacuum, however, is mission-ending because there's only one of them. The architecture deliberately puts redundancy where engines can be many and accepts a single point of failure where one large engine is more efficient.

questions.explanation: Engine-out tolerance is a major Falcon 9 design feature. It's why 9 engines were chosen instead of 3-5. The cost is interfaces and complexity; the benefit is the ability to reach orbit despite engine anomalies. SpaceX has demonstrated this in real flight.
